name: Definition of Extra-Special p-Group
statement: A finite p-group G is called extra-special if G' and Z(G) coincide and have order p.

name: Conjugacy Classes and Centralizers
statement: The conjugation action g ↦ (x ↦ g^{-1}xg) partitions G into orbits called conjugacy classes. The stabilizer of x is the centralizer C_G(x) = {g∈G | gx = xg}. The size of the conjugacy class of x is |G : C_G(x)|.

name: Definition of inner derivation (1-coboundary)
statement: For a ∈ A, define a function δ(a): G → A by g^{δ(a)} = [g,a] = a^{-g+1}. Such derivations are called inner derivations (or 1-coboundaries). The set of inner derivations is denoted Inn(G,A) or B^1(G,A).
proof_sketch: The commutator identity [g_1 g_2, a] = [g_1, a]^{g_2}[g_2, a] shows δ(a) is a derivation.

name: Free Resolution
statement: An exact sequence 0 → R → F → A → 0 of Abelian groups in which F and R are free is called a free resolution of A.
proof_sketch: As a subgroup of a free group, R is also free (Jacobson), so such resolutions exist.

name: Singular Cochain Complex
statement: A singular q-cochain on a topological space X is a linear functional on the Z-module S_q(X) of singular q-chains. The group of singular q-cochains is s^q(X) = Hom(S_q(X), Z). The coboundary operator d is defined by (dw)(c) = w(∂c). The graded group S*(X) = ⊕ s^q(X) becomes a differential complex; the homology of this complex is the singular cohomology of X with integer coefficients, denoted H*(X). Replacing Z with an Abelian group G yields singular cohomology with coefficients in G, H*(X; G).

name: Section, Frame, and Space of Sections
name_norm: section, frame, and space of sections
statement: A section over V is a map s: V → E with π ∘ s = id_V. The space of all sections is Γ(V, E). A frame is a collection of sections s₁,...,sₙ that form a basis of each fiber. A global zero section exists.

name: Transition Functions and Cocycle Condition
statement: Transition functions g_{αβ} of a vector bundle satisfy the cocycle condition g_{αβ} ∘ g_{βγ} = g_{αγ} on V_α ∩ V_β ∩ V_γ.

name: Bundle Map Definition
statement: A bundle map (homomorphism) between vector bundles E and E' is a fiber-preserving smooth map f: E → E' that is linear on corresponding fibers.

name: Jacobi Symbol Definition
statement: Let b be a positive odd integer with prime factorization b = p1 p2 ... pm, where the pi are not necessarily distinct. The Jacobi symbol (a/b) is defined as (a/b) = (a/p1)(a/p2)...(a/pm), where (a/pi) is the Legendre symbol.

name: Quadratic Number Field
statement: An algebraic number field F is called a quadratic number field if [F : Q] = 2.

name: Algebraic Variety
statement: An algebraic set V defined by polynomials f1,...,fm is called an algebraic variety if the ideal generated by f1,...,fm in F[x1,...,xn] is a prime ideal.

name: Algebraic Number Field and its Ring of Integers
name_norm: algebraic number field and its ring of integers
statement: An algebraic number field is a subfield F of the complex numbers with [F:ℚ] finite. The ring of algebraic integers in F is D = F ∩ Ω, where Ω is the set of all algebraic integers.

name: Dedekind Ring
statement: A Dedekind ring is an integral domain in which every nonzero ideal can be written uniquely as a product of prime ideals.

name: Definition of f_p for an elliptic curve
name_norm: definition of f_p for an elliptic curve
statement: For the elliptic curve E : y² = x³ − Ax − B and a prime p, define f_p = − ∑_{x=0}^{p-1} ((x³ − Ax − B)/p).

name: Definition of Hecke characters (Grossencharaktere)
statement: Characters introduced by Hecke, originally called 'Grossencharaktere'; in later terminology often called algebraic Hecke characters or characters of type A₀.

name: Weil's definition of local and global zeta functions
name_norm: weils definition of local and global zeta functions
statement: In his 1954 paper, Weil defines local and global zeta functions for a nonsingular algebraic variety defined over an algebraic number field.

name: Hecke Operator
statement: For a positive integer n, the n-th Hecke operator T_n acts on modular forms of weight k by averaging over representatives of integral matrices of determinant n.
proof_sketch: Definition via double coset decomposition: T_n f(z) = n^{k-1} ∑_{ad=n, 0≤b<d} d^{-k} f((az+b)/d). One checks it preserves modularity.

name: Non-Archimedean Absolute Value (Valuation)
statement: An absolute value satisfying the stronger condition AV 4. |x + y|_v ≤ max(|x|_v, |y|_v) is called a valuation or non-archimedean.

name: Dependence of Absolute Values
statement: Two non-trivial absolute values are called dependent if they define the same topology; if they do not, they are independent.

name: Partial Differential Operator DP on Schwartz Space
name_norm: partial differential operator dp on schwartz space
statement: For an n-tuple of non-negative integers P = (p1,...,pn), D^P = ∂^{|p|}/∂x1^{p1}...∂xn^{pn} where |p| = p1+...+pn. The operator D^P maps S into itself.
proof_sketch: By definition of S, partial derivatives of all orders exist, are continuous, and tend to 0 rapidly at infinity.

name: Multiplication Operator MP on Schwartz Space
name_norm: multiplication operator mp on schwartz space
statement: M_j is multiplication by the j-th variable. M^P f(x) = x1^{p1}...xn^{pn} f(x). The operator M^P maps S into itself.
proof_sketch: Multiplication by a polynomial preserves smoothness, and the product of a polynomial with a rapidly decaying function remains rapidly decaying.

name: Conductor of a p-adic Quasi-Character
statement: For a p-adic field, the subgroups 1 + ℘ᵐ (m ≥ 0) form a fundamental system of neighborhoods of 1 in U. Any quasi-character c vanishes on some 1 + ℘ᵐ. The conductor 𝔣 of c is the ideal 𝔣 = ℘ᵐ where m is the smallest integer such that c(1 + ℘ᵐ) = 1. If m = 0, then 𝔣 = 0.
proof_sketch: Since c is continuous and trivial on an open neighborhood of 1, and the subgroups 1+℘ᵐ form a basis, there exists a minimal m (or 0).

name: Nearest Integer Function
statement: ⌊x⌋ denotes an integer nearest to x, defined by ⌊x⌋ = ⌊x + 1/2⌋.

name: Divisibility Notations
statement: d|n means d divides n; d||n means d|n and (d, n/d) = 1; notations p|n and p^a||n imply p is prime, with p^a||n meaning p^a is the highest power of p dividing n.

name: Signature of a Number Field
statement: The signature of a number field is the pair (r1, r2) where r1 is the number of embeddings of K whose image lie in R, and 2r2 is the number of non-real complex embeddings, so that r1 + 2r2 = n. Non-real embeddings come in complex conjugate pairs. If T is an irreducible polynomial defining K by one of its roots, r1 (resp. 2r2) is the number of real (resp. non-real) roots of T in C. When r2 = 0 (resp. r1 = 0) the field and polynomial are called totally real (resp. totally complex).

name: Definition of unit, divisibility, and irreducible element in an integral domain
name_norm: definition of unit, divisibility, and irreducible element in an integral domain
statement: Let R be an integral domain. An element u in R is a unit if it has a multiplicative inverse in R. For a, b in R with b ≠ 0, we say that b divides a (written b | a) if there exists q ∈ R such that a = bq; such a q is unique and denoted a/b. An element p ∈ R is called irreducible (or prime) if whenever q divides p, either q or p/q is a unit.

name: Fundamental Discriminant
statement: An integer D is called a fundamental discriminant if D is the discriminant of a quadratic field K. Equivalently, D ≠ 1 and either D ≡ 1 (mod 4) and is squarefree, or D ≡ 0 (mod 4), D/4 is squarefree and D/4 ≡ 2 or 3 (mod 4).

name: Unit Group and Torsion Subgroup
statement: The set of units in a number field K form a multiplicative group denoted U(K). The torsion subgroup of U(K), consisting of the roots of unity in K, is denoted μ(K).

name: Definition of the Arithmetic-Geometric Mean (AGM)
statement: Let a and b be two positive real numbers. The Arithmetic-Geometric mean of a and b, denoted by AGM(a,b), is defined as the common limit of the sequences a_n and b_n defined by a_0 = a, b_0 = b, a_{n+1} = (a_n + b_n)/2 and b_{n+1} = √(a_n b_n).

name: Quotient Field of an Integral Domain
name_norm: quotient field of an integral domain
statement: The quotient field K(R) of an integral domain R is the localization R[U^{-1}] where U = R \ {0}.

name: Definition of Normal Domain
statement: An integral domain R is normal if it is integrally closed in its fraction field.

name: Family as Morphism
statement: A family of varieties or algebras is a morphism; if φ: X → B is a morphism of varieties, the preimages of points of B (called fibers) are varieties that vary in a family parametrized by the points of B.

name: Discrete valuation ring (DVR)
statement: A discrete valuation ring (DVR) is a one-dimensional regular local ring; equivalently, the valuation ring of a discrete valuation.

name: Extension of Monomial Order to Terms
name_norm: extension of monomial order to terms
statement: If um and vn are terms with non-zero coefficients u, v in k, and m, n are monomials with m > n (resp. m ≥ n) then we say um > vn (resp. um ≥ vn). This is not a partial order but a convenient notation.

name: Initial Submodule
statement: For a monomial order > and a submodule M of F, in>(M) is the monomial submodule generated by {in>(f) : f ∈ M}.

name: R-linear Derivation
statement: If S is an R-algebra, a derivation d: S → M is R-linear if it is a map of R-modules. The set of all R-linear derivations from S to M is denoted Der_R(S, M).

name: Depth of an ideal on a module
name_norm: depth of an ideal on a module
statement: Let I be an ideal of R and M a finitely generated R-module. If IM ≠ M, define the depth of I on M, written depth(I, M), to be the length of any maximal M-sequence in I. If IM = M, set depth(I, M) = ∞. When M = R, write depth(I) for depth(I, R). If R is local with maximal ideal P, the depth of M is depth(P, M).
proof_sketch: By Theorem 17.4, all maximal M-sequences in I have the same length whenever IM ≠ M, so depth(I, M) is well-defined.

name: Koszul complex (for a map to R)
name_norm: koszul complex (for a map to r
statement: The Koszul complex is associated to a sequence of elements of R, or equivalently to a map from a finitely generated free R-module F to R.
proof_sketch: Standard construction using exterior powers; not detailed here.

name: Boundaries, Cycles, and Homology
statement: In a complex F, the elements of the image of φ_{i+1} are called boundaries, the elements of the kernel of φ_i are called cycles. The homology of F at F_i, denoted H_i(F), is the quotient module (ker φ_i) / (im φ_{i+1}).

name: Module as a Complex
statement: An R-module M may be considered as a complex with only one nonzero term (all other terms zero).

name: Limit of a Spectral Sequence
statement: Given a spectral sequence, define submodules 0 = 1B ⊂ 2B ⊂ … ⊂ rB ⊂ … ⊂ rZ ⊂ … ⊂ 2Z ⊂ 1Z = 1E such that iE = iZ / iB. Let 1Z = 1E, 1B = 0. Having defined iB and iZ for i ≤ r, define r+1Z as the kernel of the composite map rZ → rZ/rB = rE → rE, and r+1B as the image of this map. Then set 00Z = ∩r rZ, 00B = ∪r rB. The limit is 00E = 00Z / 00B.

name: Coproduct as a colimit
statement: The coproduct of a collection of objects {B_i} of A, denoted ∐_i B_i, is the colimit of the diagram consisting of the objects B_i together with only their identity morphisms.

name: Coequalizer as a colimit
statement: The coequalizer of a pair of parallel morphisms ψ, ψ': C_1 → C_2 in A is the colimit of the diagram consisting of the objects C_1, C_2, their identity morphisms, and the two morphisms ψ, ψ'.

name: Syzygy Modules
statement: Given an exact sequence 0 → N → F → M → 0 with F projective, N is called a first syzygy module of M. The nth syzygy module is defined inductively as the first syzygy module of the (n−1)st syzygy module. Syzygy modules are unique up to projective summands.

name: Bounded-below complexes
statement: K+(M) is the full subcategory of K(M) consisting of bounded-below complexes F such that F_i = 0 for i sufficiently small.

name: Minimal Injective Resolution
statement: An injective resolution ℰ of M is minimal if each E^n is the injective hull of ker(E^n → E^{n+1}).

name: Graded structure on the tensor algebra of a graded module
name_norm: graded structure on the tensor algebra of a graded module
statement: If R is a graded ring concentrated in degree 0 and M = ⊕ M_i is a Z-graded R-module, then T_R(M) is Z-graded with i-th graded component ⊕_{k} ⊕_{i_1+⋯+i_k = i} M_{i_1} ⊗_R ⋯ ⊗_R M_{i_k}.

name: Algebraic Independence and Transcendence Basis
statement: A subset B of a field extension K/k is algebraically independent over k if the subfield k(B) is isomorphic to the rational function field k({X_b}_{b∈B}) via the map sending X_b to b. Equivalently, for any integer n, any nonzero polynomial f(t_1,…,t_n) with coefficients in k, and any distinct b_1,…,b_n ∈ B, f(b_1,…,b_n) ≠ 0. B is a transcendence basis for K over k if B is algebraically independent and K is algebraic over k(B).

name: Definition of Normal Scheme
statement: A scheme is normal if all its local rings are integrally closed.

name: Definition of Finite Type Morphism
statement: A morphism is of finite type if the extensions of local rings are finitely generated algebras.

name: Definition of Uniformizer at a Point
name_norm: definition of uniformizer at a point
statement: Fix a point p in Spec(R) with discrete valuation ord_p. A uniformizer u ∈ R for p is an element such that ord_p(u) = 1.

name: Vector Fields Agreeing on a Set
name_norm: vector fields agreeing on a set
statement: Two vector fields V and W agree on a set A if V(P) = W(P) for all P in A.

name: Vector Field on a Sphere
statement: A vector field V on a sphere S assigns to each point P in S a vector V(P) in the tangent space T_pS, with the mapping P ↦ V(P) being continuous. For the standard sphere S^2 = {(x,y,z): x^2+y^2+z^2=1}, the tangent space is T_pS = {(a,b,c): (a,b,c)·(x,y,z) = ax+by+cz = 0}.

name: Isomorphism of Coverings
statement: An isomorphism between coverings p: Y → X and p': Y' → X is a homeomorphism φ: Y → Y' such that p' ∘ φ = p.

name: Reduced Zeroth Cohomology Group
statement: For a nonempty space U, the reduced 0th cohomology group H̃^0(U) is the quotient of the space of locally constant functions on U by the subspace of constant functions; thus dim H̃^0(U) = dim H^0(U) − 1, and U is connected iff H̃^0(U) = 0.

name: Ramification Point
statement: A point P in X is called a ramification point for the analytic map f if its ramification index satisfies e_f(P) > 1.

name: Chain Complex of an Ordered Simplicial Complex
name_norm: chain complex of an ordered simplicial complex
statement: For an ordered simplicial complex K (partial order on vertices such that each simplex is totally ordered), CnK is the free abelian group on the n-simplices. The boundary ∂:CnK → Cn-1K is defined by ∂(v0,…,vn) = ∑_{i=0}^n (-1)^i (v0,…,v̂i,…,vn).

name: Singular Cohomology Groups
statement: For a topological space X and abelian group G, the n-th singular cohomology group Hⁿ(X; G) is the quotient ker(δⁿ)/im(δⁿ⁻¹), where δ is the coboundary operator. Elements of ker are cocycles, elements of im are coboundaries.
proof_sketch: Verify that δⁿ⁺¹ ∘ δⁿ = 0 using ∂² = 0, so im(δⁿ⁻¹) ⊆ ker(δⁿ), thus the quotient is defined.

name: Vector Space Structure on a Field Extension
name_norm: vector space structure on a field extension
statement: For a field extension F ⊆ K, K is made into an F-vector space by defining scalar multiplication for α ∈ F and a ∈ K as α·a = αa (multiplication in K).

name: Finite and Infinite Extensions
statement: If [K : F] < ∞, then K is a finite extension of F; otherwise K is an infinite extension of F.

name: Field of Formal Generalized Power Series (Laurent Series)
name_norm: field of formal generalized power series (laurent series
statement: For a field k, k((x)) is the set of formal sums ∑_{n=n0}^∞ a_n x^n with n0 ∈ Z and a_n ∈ k. Addition is termwise and multiplication is convolution. The set k((x)) is a field with these operations.

name: Purely transcendental field extension
statement: A field K is purely transcendental over a subfield F if K is isomorphic to a field of rational functions over F in some number, finite or infinite, of variables. If K = F(t_1, ..., t_n) with {t_1, ..., t_n} algebraically independent, then K is often said to be a rational extension of F.

name: Standard topology on ℝ
statement: A nonempty subset U ⊆ ℝ is open if for every x ∈ U there exists δ > 0 such that (x - δ, x + δ) ⊆ U. Together with ∅, this defines the standard topology on ℝ.
proof_sketch: Easy exercise to verify the topology axioms.

name: Metric space topology
statement: Let (X, d) be a metric space. A nonempty subset U ⊆ X is open if for every x ∈ U there exists δ > 0 such that the open ball B(x, δ) = {y ∈ X : d(x, y) < δ} is contained in U. This defines the metric space topology.
proof_sketch: Straightforward verification using metric space axioms.

name: Finite complement topology
statement: On a set X, the finite complement topology is defined by declaring a proper subset of X closed if it is finite. Equivalently, a nonempty subset is open if its complement is finite.
proof_sketch: Verification: finite unions and arbitrary intersections of finite sets are finite, so closed sets satisfy axioms.

name: Finite-Dimensional Vector Space
statement: If V has a finite basis, then V is said to be a finite dimensional vector space.

name: Stable Quotient of an Ideal
statement: The stable quotient of I with respect to f, denoted I : f^∞, is the ideal {g ∈ R : there exists n ≥ 1 for which f^n g ∈ I}.

name: Adapted Monomial Order
statement: A monomial order on k[z1,...,zm,w1,...,wn] is said to be adapted to a given linear function ℓ (presumably tailored for integer programming problems). (The full definition is truncated in the excerpt.)

name: Pure n-dimensional Polyhedral Complex
statement: A polyhedral complex ∆ ⊂ R^n is said to be pure n-dimensional if every maximal element of ∆ (with respect to inclusion) is an n-dimensional polyhedron.

name: Adjacent Polytopes in a Complex
statement: Two n-dimensional polytopes in a complex ∆ are said to be adjacent (definition continues in text).

name: Graded Resolution
statement: A graded resolution of a graded R-module M is a resolution ... → F_2 → F_1 → F_0 → M → 0 where each F_i is a graded free module and each differential is a graded homomorphism of degree zero.

name: Supporting Hyperplane of a Polytope
statement: A supporting hyperplane H of a convex polytope P ⊂ ℝⁿ is a hyperplane such that P is entirely contained in one of the closed half-spaces determined by H and H ∩ P is non-empty.
proof_sketch: This is a definition; no proof is required.

name: Radical and Radical Ideal
statement: Let I ⊂ k[x1, ..., xn] be an ideal. The radical of I is √I = {g ∈ k[x1, ..., xn] : g^m ∈ I for some integer m ≥ 1}. I is a radical ideal if √I = I.

name: Minimal Element of a Submodule
statement: Let M be a nonzero submodule of k[x]^2. A minimal element of M with respect to a monomial order > is an element g ∈ M \ {0} such that LT(g) is minimal with respect to >.

name: Order Domain
statement: A pair (R, ρ) is an order domain if ρ is an order function on R.

name: Definition of a Lattice Polytope
statement: A lattice polytope is a set of the form Conv(A), where A ⊂ ℤⁿ is finite. These are convex hulls of sets of points with integer coordinates.

name: Definition of an Affine Hyperplane
statement: An affine hyperplane in ℝⁿ is defined by an equation of the form m·ν = −a, where ν is a nonzero vector in ℝⁿ and a is a real number.

name: Definition of Supporting Hyperplane and Inward Normal
name_norm: definition of supporting hyperplane and inward normal
statement: For a polytope Q ⊂ ℝⁿ and a nonzero vector ν ∈ ℝⁿ, let a_Q(ν) = −min_{m∈Q} (m·ν). The equation m·ν = −a_Q(ν) is called a supporting hyperplane of Q, and ν is called an inward pointing normal.

name: Dirichlet L-function
statement: For a Dirichlet character χ modulo N and Re(s) > 1, L(s, χ) = ∑_{n=1}^∞ χ(n) n^{-s} = ∏_{p prime} (1 - χ(p) p^{-s})^{-1}.

name: Powers with Integer Exponents
statement: For a ∈ G: if n ≥ 0, a^n is the product of n copies of a (a^0 = 1, a^1 = a); if n < 0 with n = −m (m ≥ 0), then a^n = (a^m)^{−1}. In particular, a^{−1} is the inverse.

name: Conjugacy Action and Conjugacy Classes
statement: The action of G on itself by inner automorphisms is defined by g·x = gxg⁻¹ for all g, x ∈ G. The orbits under this action are the conjugacy classes of G; two elements are conjugate if they lie in the same conjugacy class.

name: Definition of Coordinate Ring of an Algebraic Set
name_norm: definition of coordinate ring of an algebraic set
statement: The coordinate ring C(A) of an algebraic set A is the ring of all polynomial functions on A, with pointwise addition and multiplication. The field K is identified with the constant functions, making C(A) a ring extension of K generated by the coordinate functions.

name: Zero set of polynomials
statement: Let K be a field and K its algebraic closure. For a set S ⊆ K[X1, ..., Xn], the zero set is Z(S) = { x ∈ K^n | f(x) = 0 for all f ∈ S }.

name: Algebraic variety definition
statement: An algebraic set A ⊆ K^n is irreducible, or an algebraic variety, if A ≠ Ø and A is the zero set of a prime ideal.

name: a-adic Filtration
statement: For an ideal a of a commutative ring R, the a-adic filtration is the filtration a ⊇ a^2 ⊇ · · · ⊇ a^i ⊇ a^{i+1} ⊇ · · ·, where a^n is the ideal generated by all products a1···an with ai ∈ a.

name: Completion relative to a filtration
statement: Let A: a1 ⊇ a2 ⊇ · · · be a filtration on a commutative ring R. The completion of R relative to A is the ring R̂_A of all infinite sequences (x1 + a1, …, xi + ai, …) with xi ∈ R and xi + ai = xj + ai whenever j ≥ i (equivalently xi+1 ∈ xi + ai for all i ≥ 1). R̂_A is a subring of ∏ R/ai.

name: Transcendence degree of an algebra
statement: Let A be an algebra over a field K. The transcendence degree of A is defined as trdeg(A) := sup { |T| | T ⊆ A is finite and algebraically independent }, with trdeg(A) ∈ ℕ₀ ∪ {-1, ∞}, where -1 occurs if A = {0} is the zero ring (sup ∅ := -1).

name: R-Algebra Homomorphism
statement: An R-algebra homomorphism between R-algebras A (α: R→A) and B (β: R→B) is a ring homomorphism ϕ: A → B such that ϕ ∘ α = β.

name: Definition: Lying over and going up
name_norm: definition: lying over and going up
statement: For an integral extension R ⊆ S, a prime ideal Q ∈ Spec(S) is said to lie over a prime ideal P ∈ Spec(R) if R ∩ Q = P. If additionally an ideal I ⊆ S is contained in Q, we say that we are going up from I.

name: Locally Principal Ideal
statement: A fractional ideal I of an integral domain R is called locally principal if for every prime ideal P ∈ Spec(R) the localization IP is a principal ideal of the local ring RP, i.e., IP = (a)RP for some a ∈ I.

name: Initial form ideal
statement: For an ideal I ⊆ K[x1,…,xn], the initial form ideal is Iin := (fin | f ∈ I \ {0}) in K[x1,…,xn].

name: Bilinear form definition
statement: Let R be a commutative ring and M a free R-module. A bilinear form ⟨ , ⟩ : M × M → R is a function that is linear in both arguments: (1) ⟨r₁m₁ + r₂m₂, m⟩ = r₁⟨m₁,m⟩ + r₂⟨m₂,m⟩, (2) ⟨m, r₁m₁ + r₂m₂⟩ = r₁⟨m,m₁⟩ + r₂⟨m,m₂⟩ for all r₁,r₂ ∈ R and m₁,m₂,m ∈ M.
